methods.description: This method describes the process of preparing shallow marine sediment samples, measuring their particle size distribution using laser diffraction, and performing subsequent statistical analyses, including basic statistics (mean, median, mode, skewness, sorting), textural classification, and Linear Discriminate Function (LDF) analysis, to characterize the sediments and infer their depositional environments.

steps.description: Particle size analysis of the shallow marine sediment section in the core was only possible to depths of 40 m as the sediments below 40 m depth are fluvial and consist of very coarse-grained sediments, gravels, cobbles and pebbles not suitable for laser particle size analysis. Laser diffraction particle size analysis (Beckman Coulter) was carried out on 89 samples at equal intervals of ca 45 cm at the Birbal Sahni Institute of Palaeosciences (BSIP), Lucknow.
steps.searchable_summary: Select sediment samples for grain size analysis from specified depths and intervals

steps.description: For each sample, around 5 g of sample was mildly crushed and air-dried before acid treatment.
steps.searchable_summary: Mildly crush and air-dry ~5g sediment samples

steps.description: Carbonate in the sample was removed by 10% hydrochloric acid (HCl)
steps.searchable_summary: Remove carbonate from sediment samples using 10% HCl

steps.description: After each acid treatment, sample pH was neutralised by thorough rinsing with de-ionised water.
steps.searchable_summary: Rinse sediment samples with de-ionised water after acid treatment

steps.description: organic matter was subsequently removed using a 30% hydrogen peroxide (H 2 O 2 ) solution.
steps.searchable_summary: Remove organic matter from sediment samples using 30% H2O2
outputs: Sediment samples with organic matter removed

steps.description: After each acid treatment, sample pH was neutralised by thorough rinsing with de-ionised water.
steps.searchable_summary: Rinse sediment samples with de-ionised water after H2O2 treatment

steps.description: Before particle size analysis, samples were treated with a 2-3 mL solution of sodium hexametaphosphate (Na₆[PO₃]₆) to disperse the particles and avoid coagulation.
steps.searchable_summary: Disperse sediment particles using sodium hexametaphosphate solution

steps.description: Each sample was shaken with a sonicator before analysis to avoid settling of large particles.
steps.searchable_summary: Sonicate sediment samples before particle size analysis

steps.description: Laser diffraction particle size analysis (Beckman Coulter) was carried out...
steps.searchable_summary: Perform laser diffraction particle size analysis using Beckman Coulter instrument

steps.description: To determine mean, median, mode, skewness and sorting, the particle size analysis results were analysed using Gradistat software (Material S2: Table S1; Blott & Pye, 2001).
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate mean, median, mode, skewness, and sorting from particle size data using Gradistat software
outputs: Statistical parameters (mean, median, mode, skewness, sorting) per sample

steps.description: Amongst the samples analysed, 58.4% were unimodal, 35.9% were bimodal, while trimodal grain sizes contributed to 3.3% of samples and polymodal 2.2%. According to the Gradistate software the trimodal and polymodal samples are categorised as very poorly sorted, while unimodal samples show poor sorting (Blott & Pye, 2001).
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze grain size modality (unimodal, bimodal, trimodal, polymodal) and determine sorting using Gradistat software and Blott & Pye (2001) criteria
outputs: Modality and sorting classification per sample

steps.description: The textural classification was established following the scheme proposed by Blott and Pye (2012), thus, sediment texture analysis was used to determine the downcore lithological variation of the Berada core (Figure 3A,B,C).
steps.searchable_summary: Classify sediment texture based on particle size data using Blott and Pye (2012) scheme

steps.description: sediment texture analysis was used to determine the downcore lithological variation of the Berada core (Figure 3A,B,C).
steps.searchable_summary: Determine downcore lithological variation based on sediment texture classification and depth

steps.description: To further demonstrate the change in energy conditions during sediment deposition, linear discriminate function (LDF) analysis was considered. To distinguish the process and environment of deposition LDF of Y1, Y2, Y3 and Y4 was employed following the technique of Sahu (1964)...
steps.searchable_summary: Perform Linear Discriminate Function (LDF) analysis (Y1, Y2, Y3, Y4) on grain size data using Sahu (1964) technique
outputs: LDF values (Y1, Y2, Y3, Y4) per sample

steps.description: ...where Y1 distinguishes between shallow agitated water/beach, Y2 shallow marine/beach, Y3 shallow marine, fluvial and Y4 turbidity current and deltaic deposition (Material S2: Table S2). The scatter plot of Y1 versus Y2 shows that 4% of the total samples were deposited within beach/shallow agitated environments (Figure 6A), whereas the rest appear to have been deposited under aeolian/shallow agitated conditions (Figure 6B). The scatter plot of Y3 versus Y4 suggests the Early Holocene period samples show transition from fluvial to turbidite depositional conditions.
steps.searchable_summary: Interpret LDF values and scatter plots (Y1 vs Y2, Y3 vs Y4) to infer depositional processes and environments based on Sahu (1964) criteria
inputs: Scatter plots (Y1 vs Y2, Y3 vs Y4)

steps.description: The results of grain-size analysis are summarised in Table 2 while the detailed description of the sedimentary units is given in Material S2.
steps.searchable_summary: Summarize grain size analysis results in tables and detailed descriptions
inputs: All calculated/derived grain size data (statistics, texture, LDF, modality, sorting)
outputs: Detailed descriptions of sedimentary units (e.g., Material S2)

steps.description: Unit 1 (50-39.6m) is dominated by coarse to very coarse sand, deposited under fluvial conditions. The sediments are yellow to brown in colour, angular, unsorted and poorly sorted, with pebbles ) is dominated by silt, followed by clay and negligible fine sand, with rhythmic fluctuation patterns between 8% and 17% clay distributed across this unit (Figure 5). Unit 3 (30-19 m) shows an increase in sand content compared to the underlying unit to ca 6%, while the silt contribution is ca 82% and clay is ca 11% (Table 2). Unit 4 (19-7 m) predominantly shows mud, with the sand contribution varying between 3% and 12% at different depths (Table 2). An intertidal environment is suggested for this unit based on the grain-size assemblage. Unit 5 (7-0 m) is dominated by silt, with a significant increase in sand content (1%-40%) at a depth of 1 m (Table 2). In general, the fine-grained sediments of Units 2-5 suggest deposition within temporally variable shallow marine environments with the top unit marking the transition from intertidal to supratidal to present continental conditions in the Banni Plain (Table 2).
steps.searchable_summary: Describe sedimentary units based on grain-size characteristics, downcore variation, and inferred environments
inputs: Grain size characteristics (statistics, texture, modality, sorting)
outputs: Detailed description of sedimentary units and their characteristics
